important_rules: Uses Y for bar height.
Uses DX for half width.
DY is ignored.
If POINTS is omitted, all current data points are used.
If no texture option is specified, the bar texture comes from the current default.
entries.summary: Draws a simple bar graph with bars centered on x values, using Y for height and DX for half width.

important_rules: DELETE removes selected data points from the current data, and deleting every point in a set deletes the whole set and renumbers later sets.
ALL deletes all current data points.
One of ALL, POINTS, SETS, or LIMITED must be specified.
POINTS and SETS may be combined with LIMITED to delete only part of the selected data range.
For mesh data, LIMITED and POINTS are not legal options in the same way they are for regular data.
CONFIRM and LOG control interactive confirmation and reporting of deleted points.
The manual also documents histogram deletion, but this reviewed entry intentionally focuses on the DATA subsection used by current samples.
entries.summary: Deletes selected data points or whole data sets from the current data.

important_rules: Bin edges are taken halfway between adjacent error-bar ends (DX), or halfway between adjacent points when no error bars are present.
For normal data, Y sets the height and DX sets the half width of each bin.
For mesh data, HISTOGRAM can produce block or Lego style 3-d plots.
Selection options determine which sets, points, lines, rows, or slices are included.
EXPAND only affects the plot when POINTS, SETS, or SLICES are selected, or when mesh limits have been set.
entries.summary: Draws a histogram from the selected data points; bin edges are inferred from adjacent DX error bars or, when no error bars are present, halfway between adjacent points.

important_rules: SPLINE uses a natural cubic spline and requires either x or y to be strictly increasing.
GENERAL supports repeated points and multivalued functions, and is the default fit mode.
TEXT uses the text coordinate frame instead of the data frame.
EXPAND only affects the plot when POINTS, SETS, or SLICES are selected, or when mesh limits have been set.
FILL assumes the curve is closed from the last point back to the first point, disables spline/general fitting, and forces the effective level to 1.
HIDE defaults to ON for mesh plots and OFF for normal data plots.
entries.summary: Connects data points with line segments, optionally using spline or general curve fitting.

important_rules: MORE adds text after the most recent title using the same parameters as that title.
A matching CASE command may immediately follow MORE, just as it does after TITLE.
MORE is best understood as a TITLE companion command rather than a standalone layout command.
entries.summary: Adds more text after the last title, reusing the same placement and formatting parameters.

important_rules: PLOT AXES and PLOT GRID are documented as PLOT behaviors rather than standalone command sections, so structured lookup keeps them under PLOT.
PLOT AXES and PLOT GRID do not plot data points.
If PLOT AXES or PLOT GRID is used before PLOT, HISTOGRAM, or JOIN, the later automatic axes are not produced.
3-d plots do not automatically produce axes, so PLOT AXES is required when axes are needed.
Polar-style plotting may also require a follow-up PLOT AXES because axes are not always generated automatically.
Sample phrases such as PLOT TITLE should be interpreted through PLOT options rather than as standalone commands.
TITLE[=ON|OFF] controls whether plot-generated titles are shown when plotting selected data.
EXPAND only affects the plot when POINTS, SETS, or SLICES are selected, or when mesh limits have been set.
FILL fills plotted symbols, but many symbols do not fill well and the result depends on the chosen symbol.
entries.summary: Plots data points as symbols with error bars, and can also add non-data elements such as axes, grids, outlines, or numeric tables.

important_rules: SET FONT remains in effect until another SET FONT command changes the current font.
EXTENDED and DUPLEX both use proportional spacing, so text width depends on the chosen font.
DUPLEX appears repeatedly in manual samples as a display precondition for later titles, annotations, and symbol/text showcase plots.
SET FONT affects later title and annotation appearance rather than the interpretation of plotted data.

items: affects later TITLE and annotation commands
EXTENDED and DUPLEX use proportional spacing
option_groups.name: usage notes

entries.summary: Selects the font family used for subsequent titles, annotations, and other text-bearing plot elements.

important_rules: SET GRID only affects the current plot unless PERMANENT is specified.
SET GRID must be followed by HIST, JOIN, BARGRAPH, or PLOT to take effect.
Default grid color and texture follow the axes unless they are overridden explicitly.
SYMBOL[=sym] draws symbols at grid intersections, with 0O as the default symbol.
entries.summary: Configures a grid overlay for later axes drawing, including optional intersection symbols and 3-d grid planes.

important_rules: SET LABELS only affects the current plot unless PERMANENT is specified.
If SET LABELS is issued with no parameters, the label settings reset to their original defaults.
The default label state is LEFT ON and BOTTOM ON.
INSIDE=OFF is useful with negative windows to suppress labels on adjacent interior axes.
CHARACTERS=n affects how much room windows reserve for left and right labels.
entries.summary: Controls numeric axis labels, including which sides show labels and how much space is reserved for them.

important_rules: Limits define the drawing space, but do not necessarily filter which data points are plotted.
SET LIMITS should be used before the first PLOT, JOIN, HIST, or BARGRAPH command.
If SET LIMITS is issued with no limits specified, all currently set limits are removed.
Changing limits between overlaid plots changes their scales.
SCALE n1 [TO n2] forces a uniform X,Y scale within the current window.
PERMANENT keeps the current limits until the next SET LIMIT command.
entries.summary: Sets the drawing limits for the current plot independently of the data, including direct axis limits, coordinate ranges, and uniform X,Y scaling.

important_rules: SET ORDER changes how later data rows are interpreted until another SET ORDER or a NEW PLOT resets the current order.
The listed field names determine which input columns are treated as X, Y, error values, symbols, or other supported roles.
DUMMY can be used to skip columns that should be ignored while still selecting a later column from the same input row.
Manual samples use SET ORDER both to attach DX/DY error bars to points and to treat a later column as the plotted symbol code.
In multi-column input, SET ORDER can select one column as Y and another later column as X while ignoring the columns in between.

items: SET ORDER X Y DX DY
SET ORDER Y DUMMY DUMMY DUMMY DUMMY X
option_groups.name: sample-style patterns

entries.summary: Controls how subsequent input columns are interpreted as x/y values, error terms, symbols, or ignored placeholder fields.

important_rules: SET POLAR switches later plotting and related operations to polar coordinates, using R and THETA in place of X and Y.
The angle representation defaults to DEGREES, but can be changed to RADIANS, GRAD, PERCENT, or FULLCIRCLE=n.
PERMANENT keeps the chosen polar settings until they are changed again.
When polar coordinates are on, axes are not generated automatically; the manual explicitly calls out PLOT AXES as a follow-up command when axes are needed.
The manual notes that this feature is not fully implemented, including limit-setting behavior that still uses X/Y/Z coordinates.
entries.summary: Enables or disables polar coordinate plotting and selects how angular values are represented for subsequent operations.

important_rules: SET SCALE selects scale type and default tick/label generation, while SET LIMITS selects the plotted range.
The default scale state is ALL LINEAR LABELS=6 TICKS=5.
For linear scales, TICKS=1 means there are no unlabeled ticks.
LINEAR and LOGARITHMIC scales interpret LABELS and TICKS differently.
PERMANENT applies only to the current axis, and options to its right are not set permanently.
entries.summary: Selects axis scale modes and the default generation of labeled and unlabeled ticks.

important_rules: Absolute windows use physical plot coordinates in inches and may not exceed the current plot SIZE.
SET WINDOW supports absolute, numbered, and relative window selection modes.
Positive relative windows reserve room for labels and titles, so the actual plot area is smaller than the nominal subdivision.
Negative relative windows occupy the full specified area and are useful for tightly aligned panel layouts.
INSIDE, OUTSIDE, and LEVEL=n create nested window levels.
Changing the window between overlaid plots changes their scales.
entries.summary: Selects the current plotting region using absolute physical window coordinates or subdivided numbered/relative windows.

important_rules: SET TICKS only affects the current plot unless PERMANENT is specified.
If SET TICKS is issued with no parameters, the tick settings reset to their original defaults.
The default tick state is ALL ON.
SIZE=n sets the small tick size in inches, with a default of 0.1.
LONG=n sets the ratio of long ticks to short ticks, with a default of 3.
entries.summary: Controls which axes show tick marks and how large those tick marks are.

important_rules: The title text must be enclosed in apostrophes or quotes.
If no position or TOP/BOTTOM/LEFT/RIGHT option is given, the next title is placed below the most recent title.
TOP, BOTTOM, LEFT, and RIGHT titles are automatically centered.
If x, y, and z are all specified, the data frame is assumed unless overridden by TEXT, DATA, XDATA, or YDATA.
TITLE DATA means both x and y are interpreted in the data coordinate frame, making it the direct way to place an annotation at a plotted data location.
TITLE TEXT keeps both coordinates in the text frame, while XDATA and YDATA mix one data-frame coordinate with one text-frame coordinate.
TITLE DATA is a TITLE placement/frame choice rather than a separate command family.
Sample annotations that look like TITLE DATA x,y ... should therefore be interpreted through TITLE placement behavior.
DIGITS=n controls lexical formatting and defaults to 4 when omitted.
CURSOR is an interactive placement mode.
X, Y, and Z are axis-relative in 3-D plots, and synonyms for LEFT, BOTTOM, and TOP in 2-D plots.
CASE modifies only the immediately preceding string.
entries.summary: Puts free-form text on the plot using text, data, or axis-relative coordinates, and can include lexical values or the current date/time.

important_rules: CASE must be the next command after TITLE or MORE, or appear on the same line as TITLE.
It modifies only the immediately preceding string.
It must immediately follow the target string on the same line or the next line.
case_text is a per-character modifier string defined by the FONTS section.
The documented example uses G to select lower-case Greek letters.
entries.summary: Controls the character case or font modifiers for the preceding TITLE or MORE string.

important_rules: If no parameters are specified, SET SYMBOL behaves like SET SYMBOL=NONE SIZE=2, and the symbol angles are turned off.
SET SYMBOL provides the default symbol only for plotted data that do not already have a symbol.
If SET SYMBOL is issued before reading data, the symbol is assumed for the data being read.
DOT and "  " both produce a dot.
NONE disables the symbol.
The second character selects the case or font to use.
Only symbols 0O through 9O are guaranteed to be centered.
Negative SIZE values are adjusted automatically by the window size.
ANGLE, THETA, and PHI control symbol orientation for 3-d plots.
PERMANENT makes the current symbol survive NEW PLOT resets.
entries.summary: Sets the default symbol for plotted data that do not already have a symbol, including size and optional 3-d orientation controls.
